coredata.dc:title: Novel paper- and fiber optic-based fluorescent sensor for glucose detection using aniline-functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tam, Tran Van
Hur, Seung Hyun
Chung, Jin Suk
Choi, Won Mook
coredata.dc:description: Aniline functionalized graphene quantum dots (a-GQDs) is synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of fructose. After a-GQDs are modulated using phenyl boric acid (PBA), resulting in fluorescence quenching, the a-GQDs/PBA system are employed as a fluorescent turn-on sensor for the glucose detection by the specific reaction of PBA with glucose. The prepared fluorescent sensor using a-GQDs/PBA exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward glucose. In addition, the paper-based fluorescent sensor is prepared for portable sensing devices by printing the a-GQDs ink with a commercial inkjet printer, demonstrating a superior visual glucose detection and a good sensitive performance for the detection of glucose in a blood serum and tear samples. Furthermore, the a-GQDs/hydrogel film is evaluated for a wearable sensor as well as fiber optic-based sensor for highly sensitive and fast on-site glucose detection. The prepared fiber optic sensor exhibits remarkable glucose sensing performance with a fast response, a low detection limit of 2.1 μM and excellent recyclability. Finally, the a-GQD/PBA system exhibits excellent biocompatibility and was applied to detect glucose in HeLa cells with satisfactory results. These results provide a novel approach for developing low-cost and flexible sensors for the portable and visual detection of glucose with high sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Glucose sensor
Paper-based sensor
Fiber optic sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the printed paper sensor and a-GQDs/hydrogel film
Fluorescent probe of a-GQDs/PBA toward glucose
Paper- and fiber optic-based glucose sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by The Ministry of Education ( NRF-2018R1D1A1B07047042 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis procedure of a-GQDs and its application for glucose sensor.
(a) TEM and HRTEM images of a-GQDs. (b) The XPS survey scan of different a-GQDs. XPS high resolution spectra of (c) C1s and (d) N1s analysis of a2-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of unfunctionalized GQDs and a2-GQDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of different a-GQDs samples (inset shows the photograph image of different a-GQDs solutions under UV irradiation).

(a) Photograph images of the printed paper using a-GQDs solution under daylight and UV light, respectively. (b) Photograph images of the printed paper sensor using a-GQDs/PBA with different glucose concentrations under UV irradiation. (c) Fluorescence response of the printed paper sensor for 10 successive recycle test of glucose detection.

(a) Photograph images of the a-GQDs/hydrogel under daylight and UV light, respectively. (b) Photograph images of the a-GQDs/hydrogel film with different glucose concentrations under UV irradiation. (c) The a-GQDs/hydrogel film with 5 mM glucose on latex glove.

(a) Schematic illustration of the fiber optic-based glucose sensor using a-GQDs/hydrogel film. (b) Fluorescence spectra of the fiber optic-based glucose sensor with different glucose concentrations. The inset is the enlarged fluorescence spectrum of sensor with 10 mM glucose. (c) Fluorescence change (F/F0) of the fiber optic-based glucose sensor with different glucose concentrations. The inset shows a linear curve over the concentration range from 0 to 1.0 mM. (d) Fluorescence spectra of the fiber optic-based sensor for 10 successive recycle test for glucose detection.

(a) Cell viability of HeLa cells incubated with different a-GQDs concentration for 24 and 48 h MTT assay. Confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells with a-GQDs/PBA; (b) fluorescent image without glucose and (c) bright field and (d) fluorescent images of cells in the presence of glucose (scale bar 10 μm).

head.ce:title: Novel paper- and fiber optic-based fluorescent sensor for glucose detection using aniline-functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro Nam-gu, Ulsan 680-749, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro Nam-gu, Ulsan 680-749, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aniline functionalized graphene quantum dots (a-GQDs) is synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of fructose. After a-GQDs are modulated using phenyl boric acid (PBA), resulting in fluorescence quenching, the a-GQDs/PBA system are employed as a fluorescent turn-on sensor for the glucose detection by the specific reaction of PBA with glucose. The prepared fluorescent sensor using a-GQDs/PBA exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward glucose. In addition, the paper-based fluorescent sensor is prepared for portable sensing devices by printing the a-GQDs ink with a commercial inkjet printer, demonstrating a superior visual glucose detection and a good sensitive performance for the detection of glucose in a blood serum and tear samples. Furthermore, the a-GQDs/hydrogel film is evaluated for a wearable sensor as well as fiber optic-based sensor for highly sensitive and fast on-site glucose detection. The prepared fiber optic sensor exhibits remarkable glucose sensing performance with a fast response, a low detection limit of 2.1 μM and excellent recyclability. Finally, the a-GQD/PBA system exhibits excellent biocompatibility and was applied to detect glucose in HeLa cells with satisfactory results. These results provide a novel approach for developing low-cost and flexible sensors for the portable and visual detection of glucose with high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Fast and accurate detection of glucose in blood and urine is of great relevance for the clinical diagnosis and treatment of diabetes, biotechnology, and food monitoring [1–4]. Typically, the enzyme-based electrochemical sensors are commercially used for glucose determination due to their good selectivity and high sensitivity. However, they are expensive and have poor long-term stability due to the intrinsic nature of enzymes, which is affected by the temperature, pH, and humidity [5,6]. Thus, extensive research has been performed to develop cheap, sensitive, and selective non-enzymatic glucose sensors. Various novel metal-based (Pt, Au, Ag, and Pd) materials [7–9] have been widely investigated. However, their practical applications are limited due to the low sensitivity, poor selectivity, high costs, and surface poisoning from intermediates and chloride [7–10]. Therefore, the transition metallic nanomaterials [11–14] and carbon nanomaterials are explored to develop cost-effective and sensitive non-enzymatic glucose sensor.

Compared to electrochemical sensing, such as what is used in commercial glucose meters, non-enzymatic fluorescent glucose sensors have high sensitivity and low detection limits, and present fast detection responses through simple processes. Fluorescent sensors that use carbon dots or graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit fascinating characteristics, such as good biocompatibility, non-toxicity, low-cost, and photoluminescent stability [15–17]. In many cases, GQDs were functionalized for the glucose detection, which led the reaction with glucose to form a complex that corresponds to the turn-off sensing mode by fluorescence quenching due to the aggregation of GQDs. This may lead to false results, operational complexity, and unsatisfactory sensitivity [18,19]. Therefore, it remains necessary to develop simple and cost-effective fluorescent turn-on sensors for glucose detection [20–22]. Additionally, a solid-state sensing platform, such as paper-based and thin film sensors, has attracted a lot of research interest for biological detection and environmental monitoring [23–26]. These sensors can show the unique features for practical use including lightweight, portability, low costs, and flexibility.

Herein, we developed a novel and non-enzymatic fluorescence turn-on glucose sensor based on aniline functionalized graphene quantum dots (a-GQDs). Specifically, a portable paper-based printed sensor using a-GQDs and a wearable a-GQDs/hydrogel composite thin-film sensor was prepared for glucose detection. The a-GQDs were synthesized via a one-pot microwave assisted method and exhibited fluorescent quenching (turn-off mode) in the presence of phenyl boric acid (PBA). The a-GQDs/PBA system shows a distinct fluorescent turn-on when detecting glucose with good sensing performance, even in blood serum and tear samples. In addition, the paper-based portable glucose sensor was fabricated by printing the a-GQDs solution using a commercial inkjet printer, which also showed superior visual glucose detection. The a-GQDs/hydrogel composite film was also employed for a wearable glucose sensor as well as fiber optic-based glucose sensor with fast response and high sensitivity. The as-prepared fiber optic glucose sensor performed a fast response, low detection limit of 2.1 μM and excellent recyclability. Moreover, the a-GQD/PBA system exhibited excellent biocompatibility and sensitivity for detecting glucose in HeLa cells. The approach proposed in this work provides a new strategy for developing inexpensive, highly sensitive, flexible, and on-site glucose monitoring for practical applications.

a-GQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method based on our previous report. Briefly, 3.0 g of fructose (99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) were dissolved into 20 mL of deionized (DI) water. Then, 30 mg of aniline (99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and an equivalent molar amount of NaOH were added into the fructose solution. The mixture was heated using a conventional microwave for 12 min at a power of 700 W. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained yellow solution was dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut-off = 3000, Spectrum Lab. Inc) for 2 days to obtain a light-yellow solution of a-GQDs. Different a-GQDs samples were synthesized with varying amounts of aniline (10, 20 and 30 mg), denoted as a1-GQDs, a2-GQDs, and a3-GQDs, respectively. For a control sample, non-functionalized sample (denoted as GQDs) were also synthesized following the same procedure except for the addition of aniline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the printed paper sensor and a-GQDs/hydrogel film
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, an ink cartridge of a commercial inkjet printer was sonicated for 4 h and washed thoroughly with DI water to completely remove any residual ink and then dried in an oven. The a-GQDs solution (5 mg/mL in Tris-HCl, pH 7.4) as an ink was injected in the clean cartridge using a syringe. The desired Fig.s or characters were printed on a filter paper (CHMLAB, F2140-110) using the inkjet printer and dried at room temperature. For the a-GQDs/hydrogel composite, 2.5 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Sigma-Aldrich, Mw =124,000 g/mol) were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water by stirring for 3 h at 95 °C. The chitosan solution (0.1 g in 5 mL DI water) was mixed with PVA solution and stirred for 2 h. Then, 5 mL of a-GQDs solution (1 mg/mL) were added. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture was casted onto a Petri dish or mold and kept at -15 °C for 10 h, then unfreezed at 25 °C. Finally, the a-GQDs/hydrogel composite sample was dried in a vacuum oven for 24 h.

The nanostructure of a-GQDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100 F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher) measurements were performed using monochromatic AlKα radiation (hν =1486.6 eV). UV–vis absorption spectra of a-GQDs samples were obtained using a Specord 210 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Raman spectra (DXR Raman spectrometer, Thermo Scientific) were acquired from 1000 to 2000 cm−1 with a 532 nm excitation source. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained to verify the functional groups (Nicolet IR 200 FTIR spectrometer, Thermo Scientific). Fluorescence spectroscopy (Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Agilent) was carried out at room temperature. The time-resolved fluorescence experiment was performed using a time-correlated single photon counting method with Spex Fluorolog-3 (Horiaba).

Typically, 10 μL of a-GQDs solution was modulated using 30 μL of a PBA solution (30 μM) followed by the addition of 350 μL of a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4, 100 mM), which was used as the working solution,. Then, 40 μL of different concentrations of glucose were added into the a-GQDs/PBA mixture and kept for 2 min at room temperature. Next, the fluorescence spectra were obtained under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence response was measured using various saccharides, such as maltose, lactose, fructose, and sucrose, to investigate the selectivity for glucose detection. Furthermore, the detection of glucose in blood serum (Sigma-Aldrich) and artificial tear (Unimed Pharmaceutical Inc., South Korea) samples diluted with DI water was evaluated following the same procedure. The samples were spiked with different standard glucose concentrations (5, 10, 20 and 40 mM), and each spiking level was analyzed five times.

To test the printed paper sensor and hydrogel film, 500 μL of PBA solution were added dropwise onto the printed characters or hydrogel film and maintained for 3 min, followed washing with ethanol to remove excess PBA and dried in an oven. For the glucose detection, 400 μL of solutions with different glucose concentrations were dropped onto the paper and hydrogel film and dried at room temperature. Finally, the fluorescent response was measured.

For the glucose detection using the fiber optic sensor, a-GQD/ hydrogel film on the tip of quartz optical fiber was treated with the PBA solution for 3 min. Glucose solutions with different concentration was dropped on a quartz substrate. Then, the optical fiber was immersed in the glucose solution and the fluorescent response of hydrogel was recorded by fluorescent spectrometer (PDS-1, Dongwoo Optron Co.). The glucose detection using the fiber optic sensor in blood serum (Sigma-Aldrich) and artificial tear (Unimed Pharmaceutical Inc., South Korea) samples diluted with DI water was evaluated following the same procedure. The samples were spiked with different standard glucose (5, 10, 20 and 40 mM), and each spiking level was analyzed five times.

HeLa cells were cultured in Eagle’s minimum essential medium (DMEM, Sigma) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum for 24 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). The culture medium was then replaced with 2 mL of fresh medium containing a-GQDs/PBA (0.1 mg/mL a-GQDs and 30 μM PBA), and the cells were further incubated for 4 h. Afterward, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS three times, and the PBS and glucose solution (10 mM) was added. Finally, the fluorescent images of HeLa cells were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (FV-1200-OSR microscope, Olympus) and the images were analyzed using the ImageJ software. The cytotoxicity of a-GQDs on HeLa cells was determined via the methylthiazol tetrazolium (MTT) cell viability assay method. Cell medium containing various concentrations of a-GQDs (0.5–3.0 mg/mL) was added to the cultured HeLa cells and incubated for 12 h. Subsequently, 20 μL of an MTT solution (5 mg/mL) were added to each cell well, followed by 4-h incubation. After removing the culture medium, 150 μL of a DMSO solution were added to each cell well. HeLa cells without a-GQDs were used as the control sample which was treated under the same conditions. The optical density (OD) of the mixture solution was collected three times with a microplate reader (SpectraMax M2). The cell viability was calculated using the following equation:
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
O D
t r e a t e d
O D
c o n t r o l
× 100
where ODtreated and ODcontrol were obtained in the presence and absence of a-GQDs, respectively.

The a-GQDs were synthesized using fructose and aniline through a one-pot microwave assisted method, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Fructose, the carbon source, were carbonized under microwave heating to form GQDs via inter- and intra-molecules dehydrative condensation. Simultaneously, aniline molecules reacted with carboxyl and carbonyl groups at edge of GQDs, resulting in aniline functionality of a-GQDs. The TEM images shown in Fig. 2 a reveal that the synthesized a-GQDs are well-dispersed and have relatively uniform nanosize. The high resolution TEM (HR-TEM, Fig. 2a inset) clearly shows that the lattice spacing of a-GQDs was observed to be 0.25 nm which corresponds to the (1120) facet of bulk graphite [27], indicating the good crystallinity of the synthesized a-GQDs. XPS analysis was performed to assess the chemical composition of the prepared a-GQDs, Fig. 2b. The XPS survey spectra of a-GQDs samples show three predominant peaks centered at 284, 400, and 533 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. In contrast, the unfunctionalized GQDs have only carbon and oxygen signals. The high-resolution C1s spectra of a-GQDs (Fig. 2c) show a clear CN bond peak at 285.75 eV, which is not found for the unfunctionalized GQDs (Fig. S1). The N1s spectra (Fig. 2d) of a-GQDs was deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 400 and 398.8 eV which can be assigned to –N-C = and –C-N bonds. The–N-C = O high peak indicates that aniline molecules formed chemical bonds mainly through an amidation reaction between the carboxyl and amine functional groups. The nitrogen atomic contents were calculated to be 1.7, 4.3, and 5.1 % for a1-GQDs, a2-GQDs, and a3-GQDs, respectively. The functional groups of a-GQDs were further analyzed by FTIR analysis. As shown in Fig. S2, the FTIR spectra of a-GQDs show quite distinct characteristics compared to the unfunctionalized GQDs. Particularly, the latter only display peaks corresponding to oxygen functionalities, including COH (1440 cm−1), CC (1660 cm −1), CO (1778 cm −1), and −OH (3510 cm−1). Contrastingly, the −OH peak disappears in the a-GQDs spectra, new peaks are clearly observed, such as CN (1349 cm−1), CONH (1530 cm−1), and NH (3400 cm−
1) stretching, which result from the amidation reaction between the amine group of the aniline and the oxygen functionalities of GQDs [28]. These XPS and FTIR results confirm the successful functionalization of GQDs. In the Raman spectra in Fig. S3, both a-GQDs and unfunctionalized GQDs exhibit two distinct peaks around 1350 (D peak) and 1600 cm−1 (G peak), associated with disordered structures and the sp2 domain in the graphene matrix, respectively. However, the IG/ID ratio of a2-GQDs is higher (1.12) than that of unfunctionalized GQDs (0.97), indicating the increase of sp2 domains or aromatic rings [29]. This might be ascribed to the decrease of oxygen functionalities in a-GQDs due to the aniline functionalization, enhancing the π-π stacking interaction of GQDs.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured to investigate the optical properties of a-GQDs. Fig. 3 a shows that the absorption peak at 257 nm of GQDs shifts to 290 nm after functionalization, and new absorption peak at around 328 nm is found for a2-GQDs, which is corresponding to n-π* electronic transitions related to various the presence of functionalities on the surface because, after the aniline functionalization, −COOH groups are converted to O = C-N-H bonds [30,31]. Fig. 3b depicts the fluorescence spectra evolution of the different a-GQDs samples under 365 nm UV excitation. With the increase of aniline functionalization, the emission peak of a-GQDs obviously red-shifted to values ranging from 429 to 470 nm, which was also confirmed by the change in fluorescent color of the different a-GQDs under UV irradiation (inset of Fig. 3b). In addition, the a-GQDs exhibited a high fluorescence quantum yield of 38 % demonstrating the potential of as-prepared GQDs for bio-sensing and bioimaging applications (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent probe of a-GQDs/PBA toward glucose
ce:section.ce:para: PBA molecules were employed to use as the glucose liker and to prepare the fluorescent turn-off state of a-GQDs. With the addition of PBA into the a-GQDs solution (Fig. S4), the fluorescence intensity of a-GQDs gradually decreases and is fully quenched at 30 μM of PBA. The amine groups of a-GQDs attract the boronic acid groups of PBA by electrostatic interaction, resulting in a close spatial orientation between PBA and a-GQDs. Therefore, PBA forms π-π stacking interactions with the aniline molecules of a-GQDs, allowing for electron transfer from a-GQDs to PBA and quenching the fluorescence. The glucose sensing capability was explored using the quenched a-GQDs/PBA solution. The fluorescence response of the a-GQDs/PBA solutions with different glucose concentrations is displayed in Fig. 4 a. The fluorescence intensity dramatically increases as the glucose concentration increases from 0 to 40 mM. The fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of glucose, respectively) exhibits linear relationship with the glucose concentration (Fig. 4b). The photographs of the a-GQDs/PBA solution under 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 4b) also show the fluorescence turn-on for the glucose detection ranging from 0 to 20 mM. Bright blue fluorescence is clearly observed when glucose is added to the a-GQDs/PBA solution in turn-off state. This resurgence of fluorescence can be ascribed to the disassembly of the PBA linker from a-GQDs because the boronic acid groups of PBA form negatively charged boronic ester complexes with the cis-diols of glucose [32,33]. PBA have been extensively explored for the detection glucose, since it can have a reversible reaction and a rapid equilibrium with glucose. Also, PBA has a high binding constant in the reversible reaction, which is capable for small amount of PBA to detect more glucose in the fluorescent sensors [34] The selectivity of the a-GQDs/PBA solution toward glucose was investigated using typical interfering species in real human blood, i.e., maltose, lactose, fructose, and sucrose (Fig. 4c). It can be seen that fluorescence is significantly enhanced only in response to glucose, while small or no change is observed for other interfering species. The binding affinity of boronic acid with saccharides is determined by the orientation and spatial position of hydroxyl groups. PBA can have the reversible covalent interaction with cis-1,2- or 1,3 diols of glucose to form five- or six-membered cyclic esters, respectively. However, PBA has weak binding affinity for other interfering species due to the absence of the specific hydroxyl groups. This result demonstrates that the a-GQDs/PBA sensor has high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of glucose, even in the presence of interfering species, which suggests the good glucose detection for real samples. In order to investigate the fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanisms of a-GQDs/PBA, time-resolved fluorescence spectra were measured for a-GQDs, a-GQDs/PBA, and a-GQDs/PBA with glucose (Fig. 4d). The fluorescence decay curves are well fitted to a triexponential function with three lifetime components (Table S2) of two fast components (τ1 and τ2) and one slow component (τ3) which are related to intrinsic states and edge states of GQDs, respectively [35,36]. While the similar τ1 and τ2 values are determined for all the samples, the τ3 value of a-GQDs increases from 10.58 to 14.26 ns in the presence of PBA. Most aniline groups of a-GQDs are placed at the edge of GQDs and PBA molecules have π-π interaction with aniline groups of a-GQDs. In addition, PBA has intrinsic affinity for glucose by a covalent reaction with diols of glucose to form cyclic esters of five or six members, which have reversible and rapid equilibrium. This facilitates the electron transfer from a-GQDs to PBA, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the τ3 value decreases from 14.26 to 10.56 ns with the addition of glucose because PBA detached from a-GQDs due to the formation of covalent bond with glucose, leading the resurgence of fluorescence of a-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper- and fiber optic-based glucose sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the practical application for a portable, visual, and on-site glucose detection, a paper-based fluorescent sensor was evaluated. The a-GQDs aqueous solution as an ink was printed on a piece of filter paper using a commercial inkjet printer. The images printed on the filter paper are hardly observed under daylight buy show a uniform bright fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5 a). To prepare the paper-based glucose sensor, a PBA solution was added dropwise

onto the printed figure. with a-GQDs solution. The fluorescence was quenched in the printed figure. with the a-GQDs/PBA system (Fig. 5b). When different concentrations of glucose were then added onto the paper, the fluorescence of figure enhances and clearly shows brighter blue color as the concentration of glucose increases. The paper-based sensor was also tested in real samples to assess its applicability. Blood serum (printed letter “B”) and tear samples (printed letter “T”) were spiked using different amounts of glucose (0.5, 1.0 and 5.0 mM). As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of both “B” and “T” letters were enhanced with increasing glucose concentration. This demonstrates the potential and reliability of the paper-based fluorescent sensor for the glucose detection in real samples. Furthermore, the reusability of the paper-based sensor using a-GQDs/PBA system was investigated by recycling experiments. After the printed paper sensor was used for the glucose detection, it was carefully rinsed several times with ethanol and DI water to remove the PBA/glucose complex and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, the cleaned paper sensor was treated again with the PBA solution for second glucose detection test. This recycling test was performed for ten sequential cycles with 10 mM glucose. The fluorescence profile of the paper sensor in Fic. 5c exhibits that the fluorescence of the recycled paper sensor after rinsing is completely quenched for long cycles, which is attributed to the easy separation of the PBA/glucose complex from a-GQDs after ethanol rinsing. Moreover, the recycled paper sensor exhibits remarkable fluorescence recovery without significant degradation in ten successive cycles, indicating the excellent reusability and stability of the printed paper sensor using a-GQDs/PBA system for practical glucose detection.

To further prove the practical value of this approach, a-GQDs/PVA hydrogel composites were prepared by simple mixing. The prepared a-GQDs/hydrogel (Fig. 6 a) shows the uniform and bright fluorescent under 365 nm UV irradiation. In addition, the a-GQDs/hydrogel composites have the porous microstructure (Fig. S6), which can provide an efficient diffusion pathway for the fast detection of glucose. For the visual detection of glucose, we prepared a shaped thin film of hydrogel using a mold and treated with a PBA solution. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence of this hydrogel is turned on by the addition of glucose; it exhibits remarkable fluorescence response to different glucose concentrations under UV irradiation. To evaluate the potential application as a wearable glucose sensor, a star-shaped of hydrogel thin film was directly adhered onto the finger of a glove, and the detection test was performed with 5 mM glucose (Fig. 6c). The film on glove emits a clear blue fluorescence in response to glucose under the UV irradiation, demonstrating the feasibility of glucose detection by the prepared hydrogel film in wearable sensing devices.

Finally, for highly sensitive and fast on-site detection, a fiber optic-based glucose sensor was fabricated using the a-GQDs/hydrogel film. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, after a small piece of the a-GQDs/hydrogel film was treated with PBA solution, it was attached onto the tip of a quartz optical fiber (600 μm diameter) connected to the spectrometer. The glucose detection was performed by immersing the fiber into a glucose solution. The fluorescence profile measured for different glucose concentrations in Fig. 7b indicates that the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel on the optical fiber gradually increases with the glucose concentration. Interestingly, the a-GQDs/hydrogel film also exhibits fast increase of fluorescence toward glucose and the calculated response time is approximately 0.44 s (inset of Fig. 7b). The sensitivity and linear response range of the fiber optic sensing system were determined. Fig. 7c shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 is linearly correlated with the glucose concentration from 0.05–20 mM. The detection limit is calculated to be 2.1 μM, which is superior the sensing performance of other GQD-based fluorescent sensors (Table S3). Furthermore, to verify its reliability, the fiber optic sensor using the a-GQDs/hydrogel film was employed for the glucose detection in real blood serum and tear samples. The detecting performance was compared with a commercial glucometer (Accu-Chek® Performa) in Table S4. The fiber optic sensor and the commercial glucometer show similar glucose concentration values, indicating the sensing performance is comparable in real blood and tear samples. The recycling test of the fiber optic sensor also displays no significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity during sequential ten cycles (Fig. 7d). These results indicate that the fiber optic sensor using the a-GQDs/hydrogel film is a promising approach for a fast response, ultrahigh sensitivity and versatile glucose detection system.

For bioimaging and bioluminescent probe applications, it is important to study the biocompatibility of a-GQDs. The cytotoxicity of as-synthesized a-GQDs was evaluated on HeLa cells by MTT assays. First, the HeLa cells were clearly observed by a-GQDs under the 405 nm excitation (Fig. S7). Fig. 8 a presents the cell viability with different concentrations of a-GQDs for 24 and 48 h of incubation. The results show low cytotoxicity with a cell viability > 95 %, even at high concentrations of up to 2 mg/mL after 48 h of incubation, indicating the good biocompatibility for in-vivo practical applications. In addition, the a-GQDs/PBA system was employed as an intracellular probe for monitoring glucose in the HeLa cells. After the HeLa cells were incubated in the a-GQDs/PBA solution, the confocal microscopy image shows that no clear fluorescence is observed due to the fluorescence quenching by PBA solution (Fig. 8b). When 10 mM of glucose was added the cells, the bright field image of the cells shows normal morphology of HeLa cells (Fig. 8c) and bright fluorescence was clearly observed in the fluorescent image (Fig. 8d). These results demonstrate that a-GQDs/PBA exhibits good biocompatibility and great potential for bioluminescent probe for the detection of glucose in live-cells.

In summary, we successfully demonstrated a highly sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of glucose using a-GQDs which was synthesized through a one-pot microwave-assisted method. The prepared a-GQDs show a high quality of graphene structure as well as excellent optical properties with a tunable band gap. The PBA modulated a-GQDs were employed as fluorescent sensors toward glucose. Because of the specific reaction between PBA and glucose, the sensor shows high sensitivity and excellent selectivity against various interfering species. Furthermore, a paper-based glucose sensor was fabricated by direct inkjet printing. The prepared paper sensor exhibits good visual sensing toward glucose and has a good sensitive performance to a real blood serum and tear samples. Finally, a a-GQD/hydrogel composite thin film was prepared and its sensing performance was evaluated for wearable glucose sensor. It demonstrates an excellent sensing performance even in real samples. Finally, the a-GQDs/hydrogel film was also employed to fiber optic-based glucose sensor that showed a fast response and superior sensitivity of 2.1 μM, demonstrating its potential for highly sensitive and fast on-site detection for practical applications. The findings of this work provide a novel route for developing low-cost and flexible sensors for the portable and visual detection of glucose with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Tran Van Tam: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Seung Hyun Hur: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Jin Suk Chung: Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Won Mook Choi: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by The Ministry of Education (NRF-2018R1D1A1B07047042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.129250.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A critical review of glucose biosensors based on carbon nanomaterials: carbon nanotubes and graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhu, L. Garcia-Gancedo, A.J. Flewitt, H. Xie, F. Moussy, W.I. Milne, A critical review of glucose biosensors based on carbon nanomaterials: carbon nanotubes and graphene, Sensors 12 (2012) 5996.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical sensing and biosensing platform based on chemically reduced graphene oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhou, Y. Zhai, S. Dong, Electrochemical sensing and biosensing platform based on chemically reduced graphene oxide, Anal. Chem. 81 (2009) 5603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene based electrochemical sensors and biosensors: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shao, J. Wang, H. Wu, J. Liu, I.A. Aksay, Y. Lin, Graphene based electrochemical sensors and biosensors: a review, Electroanalysis 22 (2010) 1027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical preparation of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in chemical and biological sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.J. Jiang, Chemical preparation of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in chemical and biological sensors, Small 7 (2011) 2413.
R. Wilson, A.P.F. Turner, Glucose oxidase: an ideal enzyme, Biosens. Bioelectron. 7 (1992) 165.
S. Park, H. Boo, T.D. Chung, Electrochemical non-enzymatic glucose sensors, Anal. Chim. Acta 556 (2006) 46.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of the nonenzymatic glucose sensor based on highly dispersed Pt nanoparticles supported on carbon nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.-Q. Rong, C. Yang, Q.-Y. Qian X.-H. Xia, Study of the nonenzymatic glucose sensor based on highly dispersed Pt nanoparticles supported on carbon nanotubes, Talanta, 72 (2007) 819.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen bubble dynamic template synthesis of porous gold for nonenzymatic electrochemical detection of glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, Y.-Y. Song, C. Yang, X.-H. Xia, Hydrogen bubble dynamic template synthesis of porous gold for nonenzymatic electrochemical detection of glucose, Electrochem. Commun. 9 (2007) 981.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of porous tubular palladium nanostructures and their application in a nonenzymatic glucose sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Bai, M. Han, Y. Du, J. Bao, Z. Dai, Facile synthesis of porous tubular palladium nanostructures and their application in a nonenzymatic glucose sensor, Chem. Commun. 46 (2010) 1739.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nonenzymatic electrochemical glucose sensor based on novel Pt-Pd nanoflakes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Niu, M. Lan, C. Chen, Zhao H, Nonenzymatic electrochemical glucose sensor based on novel Pt-Pd nanoflakes, Talanta 99 (2012) 1062.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Seed-mediated synthesis of copper nanoparticles on carbon nanotubes and their application in nonenzymatic glucose biosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.-M. Lu, X.-B. Zhang, G.-L. Shen, R.-Q. Yu, Seed-mediated synthesis of copper nanoparticles on carbon nanotubes and their application in nonenzymatic glucose biosensors, Anal. Chim. Acta 715 (2012) 99.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ni nanoparticles decorated titania nanotube arrays as efficient nonenzymatic glucose sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yu, X. Peng, G. Cao, M. Zhou, L. Qiao, J. Yao, H. He, Ni nanoparticles decorated titania nanotube arrays as efficient nonenzymatic glucose sensor, Electrochim. Acta 76 (2012) 512.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-enzymatic electrochemical glucose sensor based on NiMoO4 nanorods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wang, D. Cai, H. Huang, B. Liu, L. Wang, Y. Liu, H. Li, Y. Wang, Q. Li, T. Wang, Non-enzymatic electrochemical glucose sensor based on NiMoO4 nanorods, Nanotechnology 26 (2015) 145501.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient and self-guided chemo-photothermal drug loading system based on copolymer and transferrin decorated MoS2 nanodots for dually controlled drug release

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Zhang, A. Li, W. Zhao, G. Yan, B. Lui, M. Lui, M. Li, B. Huo, J. Lui, An efficient and self-guided chemo-photothermal drug loading system based on copolymer and transferrin decorated MoS2 nanodots for dually controlled drug release, Chem. Eng. J. 342 (2018) 120.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, G. Wu, G. Yang, J. Peng, J. Zhao, J.-J. Zhu, Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives, Nanoscale 5 (2013) 4015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chaotic dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.A. Ponomarenko, F. Schedin, M.I. Katsnelson, R. Yang, E.W. Hill, K.S. Novoselov, A. K. Geim, Chaotic dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots, Science 320 (2008) 356.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots through the charge transfer effect of functional groups

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.H. Jin, D.H. Kim, G.H. Jun, S.H. Hong, S. Jeon, Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots through the charge transfer effect of functional groups, ACS Nano 7 (2013) 1239.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boronic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive glucose determination in microdialysate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.-B. Qu, X. Zhou, L. Gu, R. Lan, D. Sun, D. Yu, G. Shi, Boronic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive glucose determination in microdialysate, Chem. Commun. 49 (2013) 9830.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the “abnormal” aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, Z.-Y. Zhang, R.-P. Liang, Y.-H. Li, J.-D. Qiu, Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the “abnormal” aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement, Anal. Chem. 86 (2014) 4423.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A glucose sensor protein for continuous glucose monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.V. Veetil, S. Jin, K. Ye, A glucose sensor protein for continuous glucose monitoring, Biosens. Bioelectron. 26 (2010) 1650.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots directly genetererated from electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes in alkaline alcohols and the applications for specific ferric ion detection and cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Lui, Y. Xu, F. Nui, J. J. Gooding, J. Lui, Carbon quantum dots directly genetererated from electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes in alkaline alcohols and the applications for specific ferric ion detection and cell imaging, Analyst 141 (2016) 2657.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Li, T. Chen, J. J. Gooding, J. Lui, Review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing, ACS Sens. 4 (2017) 1732.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-diffusion driven ultrafast detection of ppm-level nitroaromatic pollutants in aqueous media using a hydrophilic fluorescent paper sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Lu, J. Zhang, Y. Huang, P. Théato, Q. Huang, T. Chen, Self-diffusion driven ultrafast detection of ppm-level nitroaromatic pollutants in aqueous media using a hydrophilic fluorescent paper sensor, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 23884.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Test-strip-based fluorometric detection of fluoride in aqueous media with a bodipy-linked hydrogen-bonding receptor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ashokkumar, H. Weißhoff, W. Kraus, K. Rurack, Test-strip-based fluorometric detection of fluoride in aqueous media with a bodipy-linked hydrogen-bonding receptor, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 53 (2014) 2225.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paper as a platform for sensing applications and other devices: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Mahadeva, K. Walus, B. Stoeber, Paper as a platform for sensing applications and other devices: a review, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 7 (2015) 8345.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent hydrogel-coated paper/textile as flexible chemosensor for visual and wearable mercury II detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhang, Y. Zhang, W. Lu, X. Le, P. Li, L. Huang, J. Zhang, J. Yang, M.J. Serpe, D. Chen, T. Chen, Fluorescent hydrogel-coated paper/textile as flexible chemosensor for visual and wearable mercury II detection, Adv. Mater. Technol. 4 (2018) 1800201.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Peng, W. Gao, B. K. Gupta, Z. Liu, R. Romero‐Aburto, L. H. Ge, L. Song, L. B. Alemany, X. B. Zhan, G. H. Gao, S. A. Vithayathil, B. A. Kaipparettu, A. A. Marti, T. Hayashi, J. J. Zhu, P. M. Ajayan, Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers, Nano Lett. 12 (2012) 844.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization and dispersibility of improved thermally stable amide functionalized graphene oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Rani, M. Kumar, R. Kumar, D. Kumar, S. Sharma, G. Singh, Characterization and dispersibility of improved thermally stable amide functionalized graphene oxide, Mater. Res. Bull. 60 (2014) 143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of graphene-based nanosheets via chemical reduction of exfoliated graphite oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Stankovich, D.A. Dikin, R.D. Piner, K.A. Kohlhaas, A. Kleinhammes, Y. Jia, Y. Wu, S.T. Nguyen, R.S. Ruoff, Synthesis of graphene-based nanosheets via chemical reduction of exfoliated graphite oxide, Carbon 45 (2007) 1558.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphenol defects induced blue emission enhancement in chemically reduced graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, Y. Liu, X. Meng, T. Ding, Y. Xu, H. Xu, Y. Ren, B. Liu, J. Huang, J. Yang, J. Fang, Graphenol defects induced blue emission enhancement in chemically reduced graphene quantum dots, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 17 (2015) 22361.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots with controllable surface oxidation, tunable fluorescence and up-conversion emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, J. Zhang, X. Liu, B. Li, X. Wang, S. Tang, Q. Meng, Y. Li, C. Shi, R. Hu, B. Yang, Graphene quantum dots with controllable surface oxidation, tunable fluorescence and up-conversion emission, RSC Adv. 2 (2012) 2717.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Swelling and shrinking kinetics of totally synthetic, glucose-responsive polymer gel bearing phenylborate derivative as a glucose-sensing moiety

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Matsumoto, T. Kurata, D. Shiino, K. Kataoka, Swelling and shrinking kinetics of totally synthetic, glucose-responsive polymer gel bearing phenylborate derivative as a glucose-sensing moiety, Macromolecules 37 (2004) 1502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glucose sensing with phenylboronic acid functionalized hydrogel-based optical diffusers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Elsherif, M.U. Hassan, A.K. Yetisen, H. Butt, Glucose sensing with phenylboronic acid functionalized hydrogel-based optical diffusers, ACS Nano 12 (2018) 2283.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation of interaction between boronic acids and sugar: effect of structural change of sugars on binding affinity using steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy and molecular docking

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Melavanki, R. Kusanur, K. Sadasivuni, K. Kishor kumar, D. Singh, N.R. Patil, Investigation of interaction between boronic acids and sugar: effect of structural change of sugars on binding affinity using steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy and molecular docking, Heliyon 6 (2020) e05081.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile one-step method to produce graphene-CdS quantum dot nanocomposites as promising optoelectronic materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Cao, Z. Liu, S. Chu, M. Wu, Z. Ye, Z. Cai, Y. Chang, S. Wang, Q. Gong, Y. Liu, A facile one-step method to produce graphene-CdS quantum dot nanocomposites as promising optoelectronic materials, Adv. Mater. 22 (2010) 103.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence lifetime analysis of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Roding, S.J. Bradley, M. Nyden, T. Nann, Fluorescence lifetime analysis of graphene quantum dots, J. Phys. Chem. C 118 (2014) 30282.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tran Van Tam is a Post-doctoral research fellow at the School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Korea. He is currently investigating nano-structured materials for electrochemical and fluorescent sensor devices.

Seung Hyun Hur is a professor of the School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Korea. He received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering and Biomolecular Engineering from KAIST, Korea in 2005. His current scientific interests are carbon materials and their applications in the electronic devices such as sensors and electrochemical devices.

Jin Suk Chung is a professor in the School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Korea. He received his Ph. D. degree from the Department of Chemical Engineering, Seoul National University, Korea in 1997. His current scientific interests are graphene and related materials.

Won Mook Choi is a professor in the School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Korea from 2012. He received his Ph. D. degree from Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Korea in 2005. His current research interests include nanostructured graphene materials for energy and sensor devices.
